question: interface vs type — what's the difference?
answer:
Both describe shapes. interface can be re-opened/merged (declaration merging) and is the idiom for object/class contracts. type is more flexible — it can be a union, intersection, tuple, or alias a primitive.

Rule of thumb: interface for public object shapes, type for unions and utility compositions.
tip: Only type can express a union like 'a' | 'b'.
code:
type Status = 'active' | 'inactive';
interface User { id: number; name: string; }
deep:
What only type can do

type Status  = 'idle' | 'loading' | 'done';   // union      -- interface can't
type Pair    = [string, number];              // tuple      -- interface can't
type Fn      = (a: number) => string;         // (interface can, awkwardly)
type Keys    = keyof User;                    // computed   -- interface can't
type Partial2<T> = { [K in keyof T]?: T[K] }; // mapped     -- interface can't

What only interface can do: declaration merging

interface Window { myApp: App; }   // merges into the built-in Window
interface Window { debug: boolean; } // both now exist

type A = { x: number };
type A = { y: number };   // ✘ Error: Duplicate identifier 'A'

This is why library augmentation uses interfaces — it's how you add typing to window, extend Express's Request, or add a property to a third-party type. It's a feature, not a quirk.

Side by side

interfacetype
Object/class shape✔✔
Union✘✔
Tuple✘✔
Mapped / conditional✘✔
Declaration merging✔✘
implements✔✔ (if it's an object type)
Extendsextends& intersection

The subtle difference: extends vs &

interface A { x: string }
interface B extends A { x: number }   // ✘ ERROR — caught: incompatible override

type A2 = { x: string };
type B2 = A2 & { x: number };         // ✔ compiles... x is now `never`!

extends checks compatibility and errors on conflict; & silently intersects, and conflicting primitives collapse to never — a bug you find much later. That's a genuine argument for interfaces in class hierarchies.

Performance (the answer almost nobody gives)

Interfaces are cached by name; intersections are recomputed structurally. In a large codebase, deep chains of & measurably slow the compiler. The TypeScript team's own guidance is to prefer interface until you need a feature only type has.

The practical rule

  • Object shape or class contract → interface (extensible, better errors, faster).
  • Union, tuple, mapped, conditional, or a function alias → type (no choice).
  • Be consistent within a codebase — the churn costs more than the choice.

How to say it in an interview

“They overlap for object shapes. Only type can do unions, tuples, mapped and conditional types; only interface can declaration-merge, which is how you augment Window or a library's types. The subtle one is that extends errors on a conflicting property while an intersection silently gives you never. I default to interface for shapes — it's also faster for the compiler — and reach for type when I need what only it can express.”

question: any vs unknown vs never.
answer:
• any — turns off type checking (avoid).
  • unknown — a safe any; you must narrow it before use.
  • never — a value that can't exist (a function that always throws, or an exhausted union).
tip: Prefer unknown over any for untyped input like JSON.parse results.
deep:
The mental model

anyunknownnever
Meaning"stop checking""could be anything""impossible"
Assign to itanythinganythingnothing
Assign it elsewhereanywhereonly to unknown/anyanywhere
Use without narrowing✔ (unsafe)✘n/a

unknown is the top type (everything is assignable to it); never is the bottom type (assignable to everything, but nothing is assignable to it). any isn't a type so much as an escape hatch — it opts out of the system in both directions.

Why any is dangerous: it's contagious

const data: any = await res.json();
const user = data.user;        // user is any
const name = user.name;        // name is any
renderTitle(name);             // no check at the call site either

One any silently spreads through every downstream inference, disabling checking in code that looks typed. That's why noImplicitAny exists and why reviewers treat any as a smell.

unknown forces the check

function handle(e: unknown) {
  // e.message                      ✘ 'e' is of type 'unknown'
  if (e instanceof Error) e.message;   // ✔ narrowed
}

Since TS 4.4, catch (e) is unknown by default (useUnknownInCatchVariables) — because you genuinely can't know what was thrown; JS lets you throw 42.

never — three places you actually meet it

// 1. a function that never returns normally
function fail(m: string): never { throw new Error(m); }

// 2. exhaustiveness checking ← the useful one
function area(s: Shape): number {
  switch (s.kind) {
    case 'circle': return Math.PI * s.r ** 2;
    case 'square': return s.size ** 2;
    default:
      const _exhaustive: never = s;   // ✘ compile error if a variant is unhandled
      return _exhaustive;
  }
}

// 3. an impossible intersection
type Impossible = string & number;   // never

Use #2. Add a Triangle to the union and the compiler points straight at the switch you forgot to update — that's a compile-time guarantee no test suite gives you.

The array trap

const arr = [];          // implicitly any[] (or never[] under strict)
arr.push(1);             // under strict: ✘ not assignable to never
const arr2: string[] = []; // annotate it

How to say it in an interview

“any turns checking off and it's contagious — one any silently spreads through everything it touches. unknown is the safe version: you can assign anything to it, but you must narrow before you use it, which is why catch variables are unknown now. never is the bottom type — nothing is assignable to it, and I use that deliberately for exhaustiveness: assigning the switch variable to never in the default case makes the compiler fail if someone adds a union member and forgets a branch.”

question: What are generics?
answer: Generics allow functions, classes, interfaces, and types to work with different data types while preserving type safety. Instead of using any, a generic acts as a placeholder type that is determined when the function or class is used.
tip: Think of generics as 'type parameters'. Use extends to restrict what types are allowed.
deep:
What are Generics?

Generics allow us to write reusable code that works with multiple data types while maintaining compile-time type safety. Instead of writing separate functions for strings, numbers, or custom objects, we write one generic implementation.

// Without Generics
function first(arr: any[]) {
  return arr[0];
}

The above function works, but the return type becomes any, so TypeScript loses type safety.

// With Generics
function first<T>(arr: T[]): T {
  return arr[0];
}

The type T is inferred from the argument passed to the function.

Type Inference

Most of the time, TypeScript automatically infers the generic type.

const name = first(['John', 'Alice']);
// T = string

const age = first([20, 30]);
// T = number

You can also specify it explicitly.

first<string>(['A', 'B']);

Generic Interfaces

Generics are commonly used with interfaces to model reusable data structures.

interface ApiResponse<T> {
  data: T;
  success: boolean;
}

const users: ApiResponse<string[]> = {
  data: ['John', 'Alice'],
  success: true
};

Generic Classes

class Box<T> {
  constructor(public value: T) {}
}

const numBox = new Box(100);
const strBox = new Box('Angular');

Generic Constraints

Sometimes we need to ensure the generic type contains certain properties. This is done using extends.

function printId<T extends { id: number }>(obj: T) {
  console.log(obj.id);
}

Now only objects containing an id property are allowed.

printId({ id: 1, name: 'John' }); // ✅
printId({ name: 'John' });         // ❌ Error

Multiple Generic Types

function pair<K, V>(key: K, value: V) {
  return { key, value };
}

const p = pair('id', 100);

Real-world Angular Example

Angular's HttpClient heavily relies on generics.

interface User {
  id: number;
  name: string;
}

this.http.get<User[]>('/api/users');

Because of generics, TypeScript knows the response is an array of User objects, enabling IntelliSense and compile-time type checking.

users[0].name; // ✅
users[0].salary; // ❌ Property does not exist

Why not use any?

anyGenerics
Loses type safetyMaintains type safety
No IntelliSenseFull IntelliSense
Errors found at runtimeErrors found during compilation
Not reusable safelyReusable and type-safe

Common Built-in Generic Types

  • Promise<T>
  • Array<T>
  • Map<K, V>
  • Set<T>
  • Observable<T> (Angular/RxJS)

Interview Tip

If asked 'Why use Generics instead of any?', explain that generics preserve the actual type throughout the program, whereas any disables type checking completely. Mention Angular's HttpClient.get<T>() as a real-world example—it's one of the most common follow-up questions.

Interview Answer

"Generics allow us to write reusable, type-safe code by using type parameters instead of fixed types or any. The actual type is supplied when the function or class is used, allowing TypeScript to preserve IntelliSense and compile-time checking. In Angular, generics are commonly used with HttpClient, Observables, Promises, and reusable interfaces like API responses."

question: Name some built-in utility types.
answer:
• Partial<T> — all props optional (patch/update DTOs).
  • Required<T> — all props required.
  • Pick<T,K> / Omit<T,K> — select or exclude keys.
  • Record<K,V> — a map/dictionary type.
  • Readonly<T> — immutable props.
tip: Partial<T> is perfect for update payloads where only some fields change.
code:
type UserPatch = Partial<User>;
type Roles = Record<string, boolean>;
deep:
The ones you'll actually be asked about

interface User { id: string; name: string; email: string; age: number }

Partial<User>               // every prop optional  -- PATCH payloads, form drafts
Required<User>              // every prop required
Readonly<User>              // every prop readonly  -- immutable state
Pick<User, 'id' | 'name'>   // { id, name }         -- narrow a DTO
Omit<User, 'id'>            // everything but id    -- "create" payloads
Record<string, User>        // dictionary           -- NgRx entities
Exclude<'a'|'b'|'c', 'a'>   // 'b' | 'c'            -- unions
Extract<'a'|'b', 'a'|'z'>   // 'a'
NonNullable<string|null>    // string
ReturnType<typeof fn>       // fn's return type
Parameters<typeof fn>       // [args] tuple
Awaited<Promise<User>>      // User

They're not magic — you can write them

type MyPartial<T>  = { [K in keyof T]?: T[K] };
type MyReadonly<T> = { readonly [K in keyof T]: T[K] };
type MyPick<T, K extends keyof T> = { [P in K]: T[P] };
type MyExclude<T, U> = T extends U ? never : T;   // conditional + distributive

Being able to reimplement Partial and Exclude is the follow-up question — it proves you understand mapped and conditional types rather than memorising a list.

The Omit vs Pick gotcha

Pick<User, 'nam'>   // ✘ error — 'nam' is not a key of User
Omit<User, 'nam'>   // ✔ compiles silently! Omit's key param is `keyof any`

Omit doesn't check that the key exists, so a typo (or a renamed field) silently omits nothing and you never hear about it. Pick is constrained to keyof T and does check. That asymmetry is a genuinely good thing to know.

Real Angular/NgRx uses

// create payload — server assigns the id
type CreateUser = Omit<User, 'id' | 'createdAt'>;

// PATCH — any subset, but never the id
type UpdateUser = Partial<Omit<User, 'id'>> & { id: string };

// NgRx entity state
interface State { entities: Record<string, User>; ids: string[] }

// derive form controls from the model — stays in sync automatically
type UserForm = { [K in keyof User]: FormControl<User[K]> };

They're shallow

Partial<{ a: { b: string } }>   // a?: { b: string } — b is still REQUIRED

Partial/Readonly only touch the top level. Deep versions need recursion:

type DeepPartial<T> = { [K in keyof T]?: T[K] extends object ? DeepPartial<T[K]> : T[K] };

How to say it in an interview

“The ones I use constantly are Partial for PATCH payloads, Omit for create payloads where the server assigns the id, Pick to narrow a DTO, and Record for entity dictionaries. They're just mapped and conditional types — Partial is a one-line mapped type. The trap worth knowing is that Omit doesn't validate its keys, so a typo silently omits nothing, whereas Pick is constrained to keyof T and errors. And they're all shallow.”

question: What are enums, and what's a union alternative?
answer:
enum defines a set of named constants. Numeric enums generate reverse mappings; string enums are more readable in logs.

Many teams prefer a string literal union (type Size = 'sm' | 'md' | 'lg') — it's lighter and tree-shakable, with no runtime object.
tip: const enums and string-literal unions avoid emitting runtime code.
code: enum Role { Admin = 'ADMIN', User = 'USER' }
deep:
Why enums are contentious

An enum is one of the very few TypeScript features that emits JavaScript. Almost everything else is erased at compile time.

enum Status { Active, Inactive }

// compiles to a real runtime object:
var Status;
(function (Status) {
  Status[Status["Active"] = 0] = "Active";
  Status[Status["Inactive"] = 1] = "Inactive";
})(Status || (Status = {}));

That object is reverse-mapped (Status[0] === 'Active' and Status.Active === 0), it ships in your bundle, and it can't be tree-shaken. Hence the union alternative:

const STATUS = ['active', 'inactive'] as const;
type Status = typeof STATUS[number];   // 'active' | 'inactive'
// or simply:
type Status = 'active' | 'inactive';   // ZERO runtime output

The numeric enum hole

enum Status { Active, Inactive }
const s: Status = 5;      // ✔ COMPILES. 5 isn't a member!

Numeric enums accept any number — the type safety you came for isn't there. (Fixed for string enums.) A union of string literals rejects anything not listed. This alone is a strong argument.

Comparison

enumstring union
Runtime codeyesnone
Tree-shakablenon/a
Iterate the membersObject.values(Status)needs an as const array
Accepts invalid valuesnumeric: yesno
Debugging / JSONshows 0shows 'active'
isolatedModules / Babelconst enum breaksfine

const enum — and why it's a trap

const enum Direction { Up = 'UP' }
const d = Direction.Up;   // inlined at compile time -> const d = 'UP'; no runtime object

Zero runtime cost — but it breaks under isolatedModules (Babel, esbuild, SWC, Vite), because inlining needs whole-program knowledge that single-file transpilers don't have. Since Angular's modern builder uses esbuild, this matters. TS 5.0's enum improvements plus preserveConstEnums soften it, but the guidance stands: avoid const enum in libraries.

When an enum still wins

  • You need to iterate members at runtime (Object.values) without maintaining a separate array.
  • You want a namespace: Status.Active reads better than a bare 'active' scattered around.
  • The team already uses them consistently — churn isn't free.

The honest position

If you use them: string enums, never numeric. Otherwise a union of string literals, plus an as const array when you need to iterate. That combination gives you the namespace, iteration, and zero runtime cost.

How to say it in an interview

“Enums are one of the few TS features that emit runtime JavaScript — a reverse-mapped object that ships in the bundle and can't be tree-shaken. And numeric enums accept any number, so const s: Status = 5 compiles, which defeats the point. I default to a union of string literals — zero runtime, exact checking, readable in JSON — with an as const array plus typeof arr[number] when I need to iterate. If enums are already the house style, string enums only, and I'd avoid const enum because it breaks under isolatedModules.”

question: Explain public, private, protected, and readonly in TypeScript.
answer: TypeScript provides access modifiers to control the visibility of class members. public members are accessible from anywhere, private members can only be accessed inside the same class, protected members are accessible inside the class and its subclasses, while readonly members can only be assigned once during declaration or inside the constructor.
tip: Angular commonly uses constructor parameter properties like constructor(private http: HttpClient), which automatically creates and initializes the class property.
deep:
What are Access Modifiers?

Access modifiers define who can access the properties and methods of a class. They improve encapsulation, security, and code maintainability.

TypeScript provides four modifiers:

  • public
  • private
  • protected
  • readonly

1. public

public is the default access modifier. Members declared as public can be accessed from anywhere.

class Employee {
  public name = 'John';
}

const emp = new Employee();
console.log(emp.name);

2. private

private members can only be accessed inside the class where they are declared.

class Employee {
  private salary = 50000;

  getSalary() {
    return this.salary;
  }
}

const emp = new Employee();
// console.log(emp.salary); // Error

This is useful for hiding implementation details.

3. protected

protected members are accessible inside the class and its subclasses, but not outside.

class Animal {
  protected sound = 'Roar';
}

class Lion extends Animal {
  makeSound() {
    console.log(this.sound);
  }
}

Outside the class:

const lion = new Lion();
// console.log(lion.sound); // Error

4. readonly

readonly properties can only be assigned during declaration or inside the constructor.

class User {
  readonly id: number;

  constructor(id: number) {
    this.id = id;
  }
}

const user = new User(1);
// user.id = 2; // Error

Comparison Table

ModifierSame ClassSubclassOutside Class
public✅✅✅
private✅❌❌
protected✅✅❌
readonly✅ Read Only✅ Read Only✅ Read Only

Constructor Parameter Properties

TypeScript allows you to declare and initialize properties directly in the constructor.

class UserService {
  constructor(private http: HttpClient) {}
}

This automatically creates:

private http: HttpClient;

and assigns the constructor value.

Angular Example

@Component({...})
export class AppComponent {

  constructor(
    private http: HttpClient,
    private router: Router,
    public authService: AuthService
  ) {}
}

Here:

  • private services are only used inside the component.
  • public properties can also be accessed from the template.

Why use private instead of public?

Using private hides internal implementation and prevents accidental modification by other parts of the application.

Common Interview Questions

  • What is the default access modifier in TypeScript? public.
  • Can private members be inherited? No.
  • Can protected members be accessed outside the class? No.
  • Can readonly properties be modified? Only during declaration or inside the constructor.
  • Why does Angular use constructor(private service: Service)? It automatically creates the property and injects the dependency.
  • Can Angular templates access private properties? No. Template bindings should use public members.

How to say it in an interview

"TypeScript access modifiers control the visibility of class members. Public members are accessible everywhere, private members are only accessible within the same class, protected members are accessible inside the class and its subclasses, and readonly members can only be assigned once. In Angular, private is commonly used for injected services, while public properties are exposed to templates."

question: What is type narrowing / type guards?
answer: Refining a broad type to a specific one within a block using checks like typeof, instanceof, in, or a custom type predicate (x is Cat). TypeScript then knows the precise type inside that branch.
tip: Custom guards return 'param is Type' to teach the compiler.
code: function isCat(a: Animal): a is Cat { return 'meow' in a; }
deep:
Narrowing = control-flow analysis

TypeScript tracks what a type must be along each code path. You don't cast — you prove it, and the compiler follows along.

function f(x: string | number | null) {
  if (x === null) return;      // x: string | number
  if (typeof x === 'string') {
    x.toUpperCase();           // x: string
  } else {
    x.toFixed(2);              // x: number  -- narrowed by elimination
  }
}

Every narrowing tool

ToolNarrows
typeof x === 'string'primitives
x instanceof Errorclasses
'kind' in xby property presence
x.kind === 'circle'discriminated unions ← best
if (x)truthiness (careful — see below)
Array.isArray(x)arrays
x is Foocustom type predicate
asserts x is Fooassertion function

Custom type guards

// The `x is User` return type is what teaches the compiler
function isUser(x: unknown): x is User {
  return typeof x === 'object' && x !== null && 'id' in x;
}

if (isUser(data)) data.id;   // ✔ data: User

The catch: a type predicate is an unchecked promise. Return true wrongly and TypeScript believes you — it's an assertion with nicer syntax, not a runtime guarantee. For real external data, validate with Zod and infer the type from the schema; that's the answer for "how do you type an API response safely?"

The truthiness trap

function f(x: string | number) {
  if (x) { /* x is string | number — 0 and '' fall to the else! */ }
}
// Correct:
if (x !== undefined) { }

if (count) is a bug when 0 is valid. Same for ''. Narrow explicitly against null/undefined.

Narrowing gets lost — this surprises people

if (this.user !== null) {
  doSomething();
  this.user.name;      // ✘ Object is possibly 'null'
}

Narrowing on a class property is discarded after any function call, because TS can't prove the callback didn't reassign it. Fix: copy to a const first — const u = this.user; if (u) u.name;. A const can't be reassigned, so the narrowing sticks. This is a very common Angular annoyance.

How to say it in an interview

“Narrowing is control-flow analysis — typeof, instanceof, in, or a discriminant check, and the compiler tracks the type down each branch so I never need a cast. For custom checks I write a predicate returning x is User, but that's an unchecked promise — for real API data I'd validate with Zod and infer the type. Two traps: truthiness checks swallow 0 and '', and narrowing on a class property is lost after any function call, so I copy it into a const first.”

question: Union vs intersection types.
answer: Union (A | B) — the value is one of the types. Intersection (A & B) — the value has all properties of both.
tip: Union = OR, intersection = AND.
code:
type Id = string | number;      // union
type Employee = Person & Staff;  // intersection

question: What does strictNullChecks do in TypeScript?
answer: strictNullChecks is a TypeScript compiler option that treats null and undefined as separate types. When enabled, they cannot be assigned to other types unless explicitly included using a union type. This helps catch null-related bugs at compile time instead of runtime.
tip: Most Angular projects enable strict: true, which automatically enables strictNullChecks. Always write code assuming it is enabled.
deep:
What is strictNullChecks?

strictNullChecks is a compiler option that makes TypeScript handle null and undefined safely. Instead of allowing them everywhere, TypeScript requires you to explicitly declare when a variable can contain these values.

This prevents one of the most common JavaScript runtime errors:

Cannot read properties of null

Without strictNullChecks

When strictNullChecks is disabled, null and undefined can be assigned to almost any type.

let username: string;

username = null;      // ✅ Allowed
username = undefined; // ✅ Allowed

This can lead to runtime errors.

console.log(username.toUpperCase());
// Runtime Error

With strictNullChecks

When enabled, TypeScript reports an error unless the type explicitly allows null or undefined.

let username: string;

username = null;
// ❌ Type 'null' is not assignable to type 'string'

Using Union Types

If a variable can legitimately contain null or undefined, include them in the type.

let user: string | null = null;

user = 'John';
user = null;
let email: string | undefined;

email = 'john@gmail.com';
email = undefined;

Handling Nullable Values

Before using a nullable variable, check that it has a value.

let user: string | null = null;

if (user !== null) {
  console.log(user.toUpperCase());
}

Optional Chaining

Optional chaining helps safely access nullable values.

console.log(user?.toUpperCase());

If user is null or undefined, the expression returns undefined instead of throwing an error.

Nullish Coalescing

Use ?? to provide a default value.

const displayName = user ?? 'Guest';

Non-null Assertion Operator

If you are certain a value is not null, use the non-null assertion operator.

let user: string | null = 'John';

console.log(user!.toUpperCase());

Use this carefully because it bypasses TypeScript's safety checks.

Angular Example

export class UserComponent {
  user?: User;

  ngOnInit() {
    console.log(this.user?.name);
  }
}

Since the data may not have loaded yet, optional chaining prevents runtime errors.

Comparison

strictNullChecksBehavior
Disablednull and undefined can be assigned to almost any type.
EnabledThey must be explicitly included using union types.

Why should you enable it?

  • Catches null-related bugs during compilation.
  • Improves code quality.
  • Encourages safer coding practices.
  • Prevents many runtime exceptions.
  • Enabled automatically when strict: true is used.

Common Interview Questions

  • What does strictNullChecks do? It prevents assigning null and undefined to other types unless explicitly allowed.
  • How do you allow null values? Use a union type such as string | null.
  • Which Angular compiler setting enables it? strict: true.
  • How do you safely access nullable values? Use optional chaining (?.) or perform a null check.
  • What does the non-null assertion operator (!) do? It tells TypeScript that a value is definitely not null or undefined.

How to say it in an interview

"strictNullChecks is a TypeScript compiler option that treats null and undefined as separate types. When enabled, they cannot be assigned to other types unless explicitly included using a union type. This helps catch null-related bugs at compile time and is enabled automatically when using strict: true, which is the recommended setting for Angular projects."

question: What are mapped types?
answer: Types that transform each property of another type. Utility types like Partial and Readonly are mapped types under the hood.
tip: Combine with keyof to iterate a type's keys.
code: type Optional<T> = { [K in keyof T]?: T[K] };
deep:
The shape

type MyReadonly<T> = { readonly [K in keyof T]: T[K] };
//                     ^modifier ^^^^^^^^^^^^^ iterate keys  ^lookup type

[K in keyof T] is a for…in at the type level: for each key K of T, produce a property whose type is T[K] (an indexed access). That's the entire mechanism — Partial, Readonly, Pick and Record are all one-liners built on it.

Modifiers — add and remove

type Partial<T>  = { [K in keyof T]?: T[K] };        // add ?
type Required<T> = { [K in keyof T]-?: T[K] };       // REMOVE ?
type Mutable<T>  = { -readonly [K in keyof T]: T[K] }; // REMOVE readonly

The -? and -readonly syntax is the part people forget. + is the (implicit) default.

Key remapping with as (TS 4.1+)

type Getters<T> = {
  [K in keyof T as `get${Capitalize<string & K>}`]: () => T[K]
};

type G = Getters<{ name: string; age: number }>;
// { getName: () => string; getAge: () => number }

Combined with template literal types, this generates key names. Filter keys by mapping to never:

// keep only the string-valued properties
type StringProps<T> = {
  [K in keyof T as T[K] extends string ? K : never]: T[K]
};

The Angular use that makes it click

// Typed reactive form derived from the model — can never drift out of sync
type ControlsOf<T> = { [K in keyof T]: FormControl<T[K]> };

interface User { name: string; age: number }
const form = new FormGroup<ControlsOf<User>>({
  name: new FormControl('', { nonNullable: true }),
  age:  new FormControl(0,  { nonNullable: true })
});
// Add a field to User -> the FormGroup fails to compile until you add the control.

That's the payoff: one source of truth, and the compiler enforces the rest.

Distribution over unions

type Nullable<T> = { [K in keyof T]: T[K] | null };
// Homomorphic mapped types preserve optionality/readonly from T — and map
// over each member when T is a union.

How to say it in an interview

“It's a for…in for types — [K in keyof T] iterates the keys and T[K] looks up each value type. That's all Partial and Readonly are; you can add or strip modifiers with ?, -? and -readonly. Since 4.1 you can remap keys with as plus template literal types to generate names, or map to never to filter. Where I actually use it is deriving a typed FormGroup from a model interface, so adding a field to the model breaks the form until I add the control.”

question: What do keyof and typeof do at the type level?
answer: keyof T produces a union of a type's property names. Type-level typeof value captures the type of an existing value/const so you can derive types from data.
tip: typeof + keyof lets you build types from a constant config object.
code:
const config = { host: '', port: 0 };
type ConfigKey = keyof typeof config; // 'host' | 'port'
deep:
Two operators, two worlds

  • typeof — lifts a value into the type world.
  • keyof — takes a type and gives the union of its keys.
const config = { host: 'localhost', port: 8080 };

type Config = typeof config;        // { host: string; port: number }
type Key    = keyof Config;         // 'host' | 'port'
type Key2   = keyof typeof config;  // 'host' | 'port'  -- the common combo

Note this is not JavaScript's runtime typeof. Same keyword, different world — TypeScript decides which by position: in a type annotation it's the type operator, in an expression it's the JS one.

Why keyof typeof is the everyday combo

keyof needs a type, but you usually have a value (a const object). So you lift it first: keyof typeof config. That's the idiom you'll write constantly.

The payoff: derive types, don't duplicate them

const ROLES = { admin: 'Admin', user: 'User', guest: 'Guest' } as const;

type Role = keyof typeof ROLES;          // 'admin' | 'user' | 'guest'
type Label = typeof ROLES[Role];         // 'Admin' | 'User' | 'Guest'

function label(r: Role) { return ROLES[r]; }
label('admin');   // ✔
label('root');    // ✘ compile error

Add a role to the object and the type updates itself — one source of truth. The alternative is a hand-maintained union that silently drifts. That's the whole argument.

Typed property access — the classic generic

function get<T, K extends keyof T>(obj: T, key: K): T[K] {
  return obj[key];
}

const user = { id: 1, name: 'Ann' };
get(user, 'name');   // string — return type follows the key!
get(user, 'nope');   // ✘ Argument of type '"nope"' is not assignable

T[K] is an indexed access type — the return type is computed from which key you passed. This is the standard "show me a useful generic" answer.

Gotchas

keyof any                        // string | number | symbol
keyof { [k: string]: number }    // string | number  (not just string!)
keyof string[]                   // number | 'length' | 'push' | ... — all Array members

The as const part is essential: without it, typeof ROLES widens values to string and typeof ROLES[Role] becomes string, losing the literals.

How to say it in an interview

“typeof lifts a value into the type world; keyof gives the union of a type's keys — so keyof typeof myObject is the everyday combination. I use it to derive types from a const object instead of hand-writing a union that drifts: define ROLES with as const, and keyof typeof ROLES stays correct forever. Paired with T[K] indexed access it gives you a typed get(obj, key) whose return type follows the key.”

question: What are decorators (as used in Angular)?
answer: Decorators are functions that add metadata/behaviour to classes, methods, properties, or parameters. Angular relies on them heavily: @Component, @Injectable, @Input, @Output.
tip: They require experimentalDecorators / emitDecoratorMetadata in tsconfig (Angular sets this up).
code:
@Input() label = '';
@Output() change = new EventEmitter();

question: What does 'as const' do?
answer: A const assertion makes a value deeply readonly and narrows it to its literal types instead of the widened primitive — useful for building precise union types from arrays/objects.
tip: Great for turning a list of strings into a literal union type.
code:
const sizes = ['sm', 'md', 'lg'] as const;
type Size = typeof sizes[number]; // 'sm' | 'md' | 'lg'
deep:
The problem: widening

let   a = 'hello';         // string          -- widened
const b = 'hello';         // 'hello'         -- literal, because const can't change

const obj = { status: 'ok' };
obj.status                 // string!  -- the PROPERTY is mutable, so it widens

const arr = [1, 2];        // number[] -- not [1, 2]

TypeScript widens literals to their base type whenever the value could later change. A const binding can't be reassigned, so it keeps the literal — but a const object's properties can, so they widen.

What as const does

const obj = { status: 'ok' } as const;
// { readonly status: 'ok' }

const arr = [1, 2] as const;
// readonly [1, 2]   -- a readonly TUPLE, not number[]

It does three things at once: literal types instead of widened ones, readonly on every property, and tuples instead of arrays — recursively, all the way down.

Where it actually matters

// 1. Deriving a union from a const object (the big one)
const ROLES = { admin: 'Admin', user: 'User' } as const;
type Role = keyof typeof ROLES;        // 'admin' | 'user'
type Label = typeof ROLES[Role];       // 'Admin' | 'User'
// without as const -> Label is just `string`

// 2. Passing a literal where a literal is required
const config = { method: 'GET' };
fetch(url, config);        // ✘ string not assignable to RequestMethod
const config2 = { method: 'GET' } as const;   // ✔

// 3. Discriminated unions
const action = { type: 'increment' } as const;   // type: 'increment', not string
// A `type: string` discriminant narrows NOTHING.

// 4. Tuple returns (custom hooks / helpers)
function useToggle() {
  return [value, toggle] as const;   // [boolean, () => void]
}                                    // without it: (boolean | (() => void))[]

#4 is why every React-style tuple hook ends in as const — otherwise destructuring gives you a useless union type.

as const vs readonly vs Object.freeze

Literal typesCompile-time immutableRuntime immutable
as const✔✔✘
readonly modifier✘✔✘
Object.freeze()partly✔✔

as const is erased at compile time — it's zero runtime protection. Cast it away and you can mutate freely. It's a hint to the inference engine, not a guarantee.

It's not a type assertion

Despite the as keyword, it isn't an unsafe cast like as User — it can't lie about the type. It only tells TypeScript "infer this as narrowly as possible".

How to say it in an interview

“It stops literal widening — you get 'ok' instead of string, readonly properties, and tuples instead of arrays, recursively. I use it most for deriving a union from a const object with keyof typeof, which keeps one source of truth, and on tuple returns so destructuring gets the right types. Worth saying it's compile-time only — it's not Object.freeze, there's no runtime protection.”

question: What is the non-null assertion operator (!)?
answer: The postfix ! tells the compiler 'this value is definitely not null/undefined here'. It removes the check but does not add a runtime guard — use it only when you're certain.
tip: Overusing ! defeats strictNullChecks — narrow properly when you can.
code: @ViewChild('el') el!: ElementRef; // asserted, assigned by Angular later
deep:
What it does — and doesn't

const el = document.getElementById('app')!;   // HTMLElement (not | null)
el.innerHTML = 'hi';

! tells the compiler "trust me, this isn't null". It is erased at compile time and generates no runtime check whatsoever. If you're wrong, you get the exact TypeError: Cannot read properties of null that strictNullChecks was there to prevent.

It doesn't make anything safe. It silences the compiler.

Don't confuse the three !/? operators

SyntaxNameRuntime effect
x!non-null assertionnone — erased
x?.yoptional chainingreal check → undefined
x ?? ynullish coalescingreal check → fallback
x!: Tdefinite assignmentnone — a promise to the compiler

?. and ?? emit real JavaScript. ! emits nothing. That's the distinction interviewers probe.

The honest uses

// 1. You genuinely know better than the compiler
const el = document.getElementById('root')!;   // it's in index.html

// 2. Definite assignment — Angular @ViewChild / @Input
@ViewChild('chart') chart!: ElementRef;   // Angular assigns it after construction
@Input() user!: User;                     // the parent must provide it

// 3. After a check the compiler can't follow
if (map.has(k)) { map.get(k)!.doThing(); }   // TS can't link has() to get()

Case 2 is why you see ! everywhere in Angular: strictPropertyInitialization demands assignment in the constructor, but the framework assigns these later. It's the idiomatic use — though @Input({ required: true }) (Angular 16+) is now better for inputs.

Why reviewers flag it

// The lie that ages badly
const user = users.find(u => u.id === id)!;   // "there's always a match"
user.name;                                     // ...until there isn't. Runtime crash.

! is a claim frozen in time. It may be true today and false after a refactor — and nothing re-checks it. Prefer code that makes the compiler agree:

const user = users.find(u => u.id === id);
if (!user) throw new Error(`User ${id} not found`);   // narrowed AND safe at runtime
user.name;   // ✔ no assertion needed

That version fails loudly and specifically instead of crashing three frames later with a useless message.

The double-bang confusion

!!value    // JS: coerce to boolean — completely unrelated
value!     // TS: non-null assertion

How to say it in an interview

“It asserts a value isn't null, and it's erased — no runtime check at all, unlike ?. and ??, which emit real JavaScript. So it doesn't make code safe, it silences the compiler. The legitimate use is definite assignment, like Angular's @ViewChild where the framework assigns after construction. Where I avoid it is things like find(...)! — that's a claim that's true until someone refactors. I'd rather narrow with an explicit throw, so it fails loudly with a useful message.”

question: What is a discriminated (tagged) union?
answer: A union of object types that share a common literal tag field. Switching on that tag lets TypeScript narrow to the exact member, giving type-safe, exhaustive handling — great for state machines and Redux/NgRx actions.
tip: Add a `never` default case so the compiler errors if you forget a variant.
deep:
The pattern

type State =
  | { status: 'idle' }
  | { status: 'loading' }
  | { status: 'success'; data: User[] }      // data ONLY exists here
  | { status: 'error';   message: string };  // message ONLY exists here

function render(s: State) {
  switch (s.status) {
    case 'idle':    return 'Start';
    case 'loading': return 'Spinner';
    case 'success': return s.data.length;      // ✔ s.data known to exist
    case 'error':   return s.message;          // ✔ s.message known
  }
}

The discriminant is the shared literal field (status). Checking it narrows the union to exactly one member, and each member's own fields become available.

Why this beats the "bag of optionals"

// ✘ The shape everyone writes first
interface State {
  loading: boolean;
  data?: User[];
  error?: string;
}

This type permits impossible states: { loading: true, data: [...], error: 'x' } type-checks fine. So every consumer must defensively handle combinations that should never exist, and you get bugs like a spinner rendering on top of an error. The discriminated union makes those states unrepresentable — the compiler rejects them.

That phrase — "make illegal states unrepresentable" — is the point of the question.

Exhaustiveness: the compiler as your checklist

function render(s: State): string {
  switch (s.status) {
    case 'idle':    return 'Start';
    case 'loading': return 'Spinner';
    case 'success': return String(s.data.length);
    case 'error':   return s.message;
    default:
      const _exhaustive: never = s;   // ✘ if a variant is unhandled
      throw new Error(`Unhandled: ${JSON.stringify(s)}`);
  }
}

Add { status: 'refreshing' } to the union and this fails to compile — s is no longer never. The compiler points at every switch that needs updating. Adding a variant becomes a guided refactor instead of a hunt.

(Alternatively, a : string return type plus noImplicitReturns catches it too.)

Where you've already used it

  • NgRx actions — the union of all actions discriminated by type. That's exactly why a reducer's action.payload is correctly typed per case.
  • Redux, and any request state machine.
  • API responses: { ok: true; data } | { ok: false; error }.

The requirement

The discriminant must be a literal type — 'success', not string. A status: string field narrows nothing, which is why as const and literal unions matter here.

How to say it in an interview

“A union of object types sharing a literal tag, so checking the tag narrows to exactly one member and its fields. The value is that it makes illegal states unrepresentable — the usual { loading, data?, error? } shape permits loading and error at once, and every consumer has to defend against it. Pair it with a never assignment in the default case and the compiler tells you every place to update when you add a variant. NgRx's action union is exactly this pattern.”

question: What are conditional types?
answer: Types that branch based on a condition: T extends U ? X : Y. They power much of the standard library (e.g. Exclude, ReturnType) and let generic types adapt to their inputs.
tip: Combine with `infer` to extract a type from within another.
code:
type NonNull<T> = T extends null | undefined ? never : T;
type A = NonNull<string | null>; // string
deep:
A ternary for types

type IsString<T> = T extends string ? 'yes' : 'no';
type A = IsString<'hi'>;   // 'yes'
type B = IsString<42>;     // 'no'

T extends U here means "is T assignable to U?" — not class inheritance.

The behaviour that trips everyone: distribution

type ToArray<T> = T extends any ? T[] : never;
type R = ToArray<string | number>;
// NOT (string | number)[]
// IS  string[] | number[]

When the checked type is a naked type parameter and you pass a union, the conditional distributes over each member and re-unions the results. This is how Exclude works:

type Exclude<T, U> = T extends U ? never : T;

Exclude<'a'|'b'|'c', 'a'>
// distributes: ('a' extends 'a' ? never : 'a')   -> never
//            | ('b' extends 'a' ? never : 'b')   -> 'b'
//            | ('c' extends 'a' ? never : 'c')   -> 'c'
// = 'b' | 'c'      (never vanishes from a union)

Turning distribution off

type NoDist<T> = [T] extends [any] ? T[] : never;
type R2 = NoDist<string | number>;   // (string | number)[]

Wrapping both sides in a tuple makes the parameter non-naked, so it's checked as a whole. That's the trick behind IsNever and most "type-level equality" helpers.

With infer

type ElementOf<T> = T extends (infer U)[] ? U : never;
type E = ElementOf<string[]>;   // string

// That's how the built-ins are written:
type ReturnType<T> = T extends (...a: any) => infer R ? R : never;
type Awaited<T>    = T extends Promise<infer U> ? Awaited<U> : T;  // recursive

Where it's actually useful

// Unwrap an NgRx action's props
type PropsOf<T> = T extends ActionCreator<any, (...a: any) => infer R> ? R : never;

// Type an API client from a route map
type Response<R extends keyof Routes> = Routes[R] extends { res: infer T } ? T : never;

The honest framing

You write these rarely — mostly in library code or a shared types file. You read them constantly, because that's what the built-in utility types are. Claiming you use conditional types daily in app code is a red flag; saying "I reach for them when building a typed API layer" is credible.

How to say it in an interview

“A ternary at the type level — T extends U ? X : Y, where extends means assignable. The behaviour that matters is distribution: with a naked type parameter and a union, it applies to each member and re-unions the result, which is exactly how Exclude works. Wrap it in a tuple to opt out. Combined with infer you get ReturnType and Awaited. I mostly write them in shared type utilities rather than day-to-day app code.”

question: What does the infer keyword do?
answer: Inside a conditional type, infer captures a type from a position so you can reuse it — e.g. extract a function's return type or an array's element type.
tip: infer is how ReturnType<T> and Parameters<T> are implemented.
code:
type Return<T> = T extends (...args: any[]) => infer R ? R : never;
type R = Return<() => number>; // number
deep:
What it does

infer declares a type variable to be filled in by pattern matching, inside the extends clause of a conditional type. It's destructuring, at the type level.

type ElementOf<T> = T extends (infer U)[] ? U : never;
//                                  ^^^^^^^ "whatever the element type is, call it U"

ElementOf<string[]>   // string
ElementOf<number>     // never — didn't match the pattern

The built-ins are all just infer

type ReturnType<T>  = T extends (...args: any) => infer R ? R : never;
type Parameters<T>  = T extends (...args: infer P) => any ? P : never;
type Awaited<T>     = T extends Promise<infer U> ? Awaited<U> : T;   // recursive!
type InstanceType<T> = T extends new (...a: any) => infer R ? R : never;

Knowing ReturnType is one line of infer is the point of the question — these aren't compiler magic.

Multiple and nested infers

type First<T> = T extends [infer F, ...any[]] ? F : never;
type Last<T>  = T extends [...any[], infer L] ? L : never;
First<[string, number, boolean]>   // string
Last<[string, number, boolean]>    // boolean

// Deeply unwrap nested promises
type DeepAwait<T> = T extends Promise<infer U> ? DeepAwait<U> : T;
DeepAwait<Promise<Promise<string>>>   // string

Template literal inference (TS 4.1+)

type RouteParam<T> = T extends `${string}/:${infer P}` ? P : never;
RouteParam<'/users/:id'>   // 'id'

This is how typed router libraries extract params from a path string — a genuinely impressive, concrete example.

The rule that catches people

infer is only legal inside the extends clause of a conditional type. You can't use it in a type alias body or a mapped type directly.

type Bad<T> = infer U;   // ✘ 'infer' declarations are only permitted
                         //    in the 'extends' clause of a conditional type

Multiple candidates

type Both<T> = T extends { a: infer U; b: infer U } ? U : never;
Both<{ a: string; b: number }>   // string | number — union for covariant positions

How to say it in an interview

“It's pattern matching in a conditional type — you write the shape you expect and infer captures a piece of it. ReturnType is literally T extends (...a: any) => infer R ? R : never. It only works inside the extends clause of a conditional. It gets powerful with recursion and template literals — you can pull 'id' out of '/users/:id' at the type level, which is how typed routers work.”

question: What is an index signature?
answer: Declares that an object can have arbitrary keys of a given type: { [key: string]: number }. Useful for dictionaries when keys aren't known ahead of time. Record<K, V> is the shorthand.
tip: Prefer Record<string, T> for readability over a raw index signature.
code:
interface Scores { [player: string]: number; }
const s: Scores = { alice: 10, bob: 7 };

question: What are tuple types in TypeScript?
answer: A tuple is a special type of array where the number of elements and the type of each element are fixed. Unlike normal arrays, each position in a tuple has its own predefined type.
tip: Use tuples when the order and number of values matter, such as returning multiple values from a function. If all elements are the same type, use an array instead.
deep:
What is a Tuple?

A tuple is a fixed-length array where each element has a predefined type.

Unlike a normal array, every position in a tuple has its own type.

const person: [string, number] = ['John', 25];

Here:

  • First element must be a string.
  • Second element must be a number.

Tuple vs Array

TupleArray
Fixed number of elementsAny number of elements
Each position has its own typeAll elements usually share the same type
Order mattersOrder usually doesn't matter

Example of an Array

const scores: number[] = [90, 85, 70];

Every element must be a number.

Example of a Tuple

const employee: [string, number] = ['John', 25];

The first element is always a string and the second is always a number.

Named Tuples

TypeScript allows naming tuple elements to improve readability.

type Result = [success: boolean, message: string];

const result: Result = [true, 'Saved'];

The labels are only for documentation and IntelliSense—they do not exist at runtime.

Optional Elements

type User = [string, number?];

const u1: User = ['John'];
const u2: User = ['John', 25];

The second element is optional.

Rest Elements

type Scores = [string, ...number[]];

const scores: Scores = ['John', 90, 80, 95];

The tuple starts with a string followed by any number of numbers.

Returning Multiple Values

Tuples are commonly used when a function needs to return more than one value.

function divide(a: number, b: number): [number, number] {
  return [Math.floor(a / b), a % b];
}

const [quotient, remainder] = divide(10, 3);

React Example

The useState() hook returns a tuple.

const [count, setCount] = useState(0);

Its simplified type is:

[number, Dispatch<SetStateAction<number>>]

Angular Example

Although Angular developers use objects more frequently, tuples are useful when returning multiple related values from helper functions.

function getCoordinates(): [number, number] {
  return [12.97, 77.59];
}

const [lat, lng] = getCoordinates();

Destructuring Tuples

const user: [string, number] = ['John', 25];

const [name, age] = user;

console.log(name);
console.log(age);

When should you use Tuples?

  • Returning multiple values from a function.
  • Representing coordinates (x, y).
  • Representing key-value pairs.
  • Representing status-message pairs.
  • Representing fixed-size records.

When should you use Arrays?

  • Lists of users.
  • Product collections.
  • Scores.
  • Dynamic collections of the same type.

Common Interview Questions

  • What is a tuple? A fixed-length array where each position has a predefined type.
  • How is a tuple different from an array? Tuples have fixed types and positions, while arrays usually contain elements of the same type.
  • Can tuples have optional elements? Yes.
  • Can tuples have rest elements? Yes.
  • Where are tuples commonly used? Functions returning multiple values, React's useState(), coordinates, and key-value pairs.

How to say it in an interview

"A tuple is a fixed-length array where each position has a predefined type. Unlike normal arrays, tuples preserve both the order and the type of each element. They are commonly used when returning multiple related values from a function or representing structured data like coordinates, status-message pairs, and React's useState return value."

question: What are function overloads in TypeScript?
answer: Function overloads allow a function to have multiple call signatures while sharing a single implementation. Each overload describes a different way the function can be called, enabling TypeScript to infer the correct parameter and return types based on the arguments passed.
tip: Use overloads when the return type depends on the input type. If a union type or generic can express the same behavior clearly, prefer those because they are usually simpler.
deep:
What are Function Overloads?

Function overloads allow a single function to support multiple parameter types while providing different return types depending on how the function is called.

The compiler chooses the correct overload based on the arguments passed.

Structure of Function Overloads

A function overload consists of:

  1. One or more overload signatures.
  2. One implementation signature.
function greet(name: string): string;
function greet(age: number): string;

function greet(value: string | number): string {
  return `Hello ${value}`;
}

The first two declarations are overload signatures. The last function contains the actual implementation.

How it Works

function square(value: number): number;
function square(values: number[]): number[];

function square(value: number | number[]) {
  if (Array.isArray(value)) {
    return value.map(v => v * v);
  }

  return value * value;
}
const a = square(5);        // number
const b = square([1,2,3]);  // number[]

TypeScript automatically infers the correct return type from the overload signature.

Why not just use Union Types?

function square(value: number | number[]): number | number[] {
  // ...
}

This works, but the return type becomes:

number | number[]

The caller must perform additional type checking.

Using overloads:

const a = square(5);      // number
const b = square([1,2]);  // number[]

The compiler already knows the exact return type.

Real-world Example

interface User {
  id: number;
  name: string;
}

function getUser(id: number): User;
function getUser(ids: number[]): User[];

function getUser(value: number | number[]) {
  if (Array.isArray(value)) {
    return value.map(id => ({ id, name: `User ${id}` }));
  }

  return {
    id: value,
    name: `User ${value}`
  };
}

Rules for Function Overloads

  • Only one implementation is allowed.
  • The implementation must satisfy every overload signature.
  • Only overload signatures are visible to callers.
  • The implementation signature is not part of the public API.

Overloads vs Union Types

Function OverloadsUnion Types
Multiple call signatures.Single signature.
Precise return types.Usually returns a union type.
Better IntelliSense.Simpler for straightforward cases.
Best when return type depends on arguments.Best when behavior is the same.

Overloads vs Generics

Use OverloadsUse Generics
Different parameter combinations.Same logic for different types.
Different return types.Preserve the input type.
Behavior changes based on arguments.Behavior remains the same.

Common Interview Questions

  • What are function overloads? Multiple function signatures with one implementation.
  • Why use overloads? To provide precise parameter and return types.
  • Can a function have multiple implementations? No. Only one implementation is allowed.
  • When should you prefer overloads over union types? When different inputs produce different return types.
  • When should you use generics instead? When the same logic works for different types and you want to preserve type information.

Angular Example

Many Angular and TypeScript library APIs use overloads. For example, methods in HttpClient expose multiple overloads so that the return type changes based on options such as observe or responseType.

this.http.get('/api/users');
this.http.get('/api/users', {
  observe: 'response'
});

Although both calls use the same method, they return different types because of function overloads.

How to say it in an interview

"Function overloads allow a function to have multiple call signatures while sharing a single implementation. They provide better type safety because TypeScript can infer different return types based on the arguments. I use overloads when different parameter combinations produce different return types, whereas I prefer generics or union types when the behavior remains the same."

question: What is the difference between an Abstract Class and an Interface in TypeScript?
answer: An abstract class is a base class that cannot be instantiated directly. It can contain both implemented methods and abstract methods that subclasses must implement. An interface defines only the structure (contract) of an object or class and does not contain implementation. Use an abstract class when you want to share common behavior, and use an interface when you only want to enforce a contract.
tip: Remember: Abstract Class = 'What it is + how it works'. Interface = 'What it should look like'.
deep:
What is an Abstract Class?

An abstract class is a special class that cannot be instantiated directly. It acts as a base class for other classes.

An abstract class can contain:

  • Abstract methods (without implementation)
  • Concrete methods (with implementation)
  • Properties
  • Constructors
abstract class Animal {
  abstract makeSound(): void;

  eat() {
    console.log('Eating');
  }
}

Since Animal is abstract, it cannot be instantiated.

// Error
const animal = new Animal();

Extending an Abstract Class

Every subclass must implement all abstract methods.

class Dog extends Animal {
  makeSound() {
    console.log('Bark');
  }
}

What is an Interface?

An interface defines a contract that a class or object must follow. It only describes the structure and contains no implementation.

interface Animal {
  makeSound(): void;
}
class Dog implements Animal {
  makeSound() {
    console.log('Bark');
  }
}

Interfaces disappear after TypeScript compilation and do not exist at runtime.

Key Differences

FeatureAbstract ClassInterface
Can have method implementation✅ Yes❌ No
Can have constructors✅ Yes❌ No
Can have properties✅ YesOnly declarations
Can have abstract methods✅ YesAll methods are declarations
Exists at runtime✅ Yes❌ No
Inheritance keywordextendsimplements

When should you use an Abstract Class?

  • When multiple classes share common logic.
  • When you want to provide default implementations.
  • When subclasses must override specific methods.
  • When creating reusable base classes.
abstract class Vehicle {
  start() {
    console.log('Engine Started');
  }

  abstract drive(): void;
}

When should you use an Interface?

  • When you only want to define a contract.
  • When different classes should follow the same structure.
  • When defining DTOs, API models, or object shapes.
interface User {
  id: number;
  name: string;
}

Angular Example

Interfaces are used much more frequently than abstract classes in Angular.

export interface User {
  id: number;
  name: string;
}

Abstract classes are commonly used for shared functionality.

export abstract class BaseComponent {
  loading = false;

  showLoader() {
    this.loading = true;
  }
}
export class DashboardComponent extends BaseComponent {
}

Can a class implement multiple interfaces?

Yes.

interface Flyable {
  fly(): void;
}

interface Swimmable {
  swim(): void;
}

class Duck implements Flyable, Swimmable {
  fly() {}
  swim() {}
}

Can a class extend multiple abstract classes?

No. TypeScript supports single inheritance only.

class Dog extends Animal {
}

Common Interview Questions

  • What is an abstract class? A class that cannot be instantiated and can contain both implemented and abstract methods.
  • Can an abstract class have a constructor? Yes.
  • Can an interface have implementation? No.
  • Can a class implement multiple interfaces? Yes.
  • Can a class extend multiple abstract classes? No.
  • Which is used more in Angular? Interfaces are more common for models and API responses, while abstract classes are used for shared component or service logic.

How to say it in an interview

"An interface defines a contract, while an abstract class provides both a contract and shared implementation. I use interfaces for models, DTOs, and API responses because they only describe structure. I use abstract classes when multiple components or services need common functionality, such as shared methods or properties. A class can implement multiple interfaces but can extend only one abstract class."

question: What does the satisfies operator do?
answer: (TS 4.9+) satisfies checks that a value matches a type without widening it — you keep the precise inferred literal types while still validating the shape. Best of both worlds vs a plain annotation.
tip: Use satisfies for config objects so keys/values keep their literal types.
code:
const routes = { home: '/', about: '/about' } satisfies Record<string, string>;
// routes.home is '/', not just string
deep:
The problem it solves

Before TS 4.9 you had to choose between validation and precise inference. You couldn't have both.

type Config = Record<string, string | number>;

// Option A: annotate -> validated, but inference is DESTROYED
const a: Config = { host: 'localhost', port: 8080 };
a.host.toUpperCase();   // ✘ string | number — the literal type is gone
a.hosts;                // ✘ typo NOT caught? (it is here, but keys widen to string)

// Option B: no annotation -> precise, but NOT validated
const b = { host: 'localhost', port: 8080 };
b.host.toUpperCase();   // ✔ string
// but nothing checks b against Config — a wrong value type slips through

// Option C (4.9+): satisfies -> BOTH
const c = { host: 'localhost', port: 8080 } satisfies Config;
c.host.toUpperCase();   // ✔ string  — literal types kept
c.port.toFixed();       // ✔ number
// and it IS checked against Config

The rule

satisfies checks the value against the type but keeps the inferred type as the variable's type. An annotation does the opposite: it checks, then replaces the type with the annotation, widening everything.

The example that sells it

type Routes = Record<string, { path: string; roles: string[] }>;

const routes = {
  home:  { path: '/',      roles: ['user'] },
  admin: { path: '/admin', roles: ['admin'] }
} satisfies Routes;

routes.home;      // ✔ known key — autocomplete works
routes.hom;       // ✘ Property 'hom' does not exist   ← annotation would ALLOW this
type Key = keyof typeof routes;   // 'home' | 'admin'  ← annotation gives `string`

With const routes: Routes, the key type becomes string — so typos compile and keyof is useless. satisfies keeps the exact keys and validates the shape.

satisfies vs as vs annotation

Checks the value?Keeps narrow inference?Can lie?
const x: T = v✔✘ (widens to T)✘
const x = v as T✘ (asserts)✘✔ unsafe
const x = v satisfies T✔✔✘

Key distinction: as is an assertion — you're overriding the compiler and it can be wrong at runtime. satisfies is a check — it can only ever reject, never lie.

Combining with as const

const config = { method: 'GET', retries: 3 } as const satisfies RequestConfig;
// as const  -> literal + readonly
// satisfies -> validated against RequestConfig

How to say it in an interview

“Before 4.9 you chose between validating a config object and keeping its precise inferred types — annotating widens everything, so keyof typeof gives you string and typos compile. satisfies checks the value against the type but leaves the inferred type in place, so you get both. And unlike as it can't lie — it's a check, not an assertion.”

question: What are Template Literal Types in TypeScript?
answer: Template Literal Types allow you to create new string literal types by combining existing literal types using JavaScript template string syntax. They help generate strongly typed strings, such as event names, CSS classes, API routes, and object keys.
tip: Think of them as string templates that work at the type level. They generate valid string combinations during compilation, not at runtime.
deep:
What are Template Literal Types?

Template Literal Types use the same syntax as JavaScript template strings, but instead of creating string values, they create new types.

type Size = 'sm' | 'lg';

type Button = `btn-${Size}`;

The resulting type becomes:

'btn-sm' | 'btn-lg'

TypeScript generates every valid combination automatically.

Basic Example

type Color = 'red' | 'blue';

type BgColor = `bg-${Color}`;

The generated type is:

'bg-red' | 'bg-blue'

Combining Multiple Unions

type Size = 'sm' | 'lg';
type Color = 'red' | 'blue';

type ClassName = `${Size}-${Color}`;

Result:

'sm-red'
'sm-blue'
'lg-red'
'lg-blue'

TypeScript produces the Cartesian product of all possible combinations.

Using Built-in String Utility Types

Template Literal Types work well with built-in string manipulation utilities.

type Event = 'click' | 'hover';

type Handler = `on${Capitalize}`;

Result:

'onClick' | 'onHover'

Other useful utilities include Uppercase, Lowercase and Uncapitalize.

Typing CSS Classes

type Size = 'sm' | 'md' | 'lg';

type ButtonClass = `btn-${Size}`;
const cls: ButtonClass = 'btn-sm';
// const cls: ButtonClass = 'btn-xl';
// ❌ Error

Typing Event Names

type EventName = 'click' | 'change';

type Listener = `on${Capitalize}`;

Result:

'onClick' | 'onChange'

Real-world Angular Example

Suppose your application supports multiple themes.

type Theme = 'light' | 'dark';

type CssClass = `theme-${Theme}`;
const theme: CssClass = 'theme-dark';

This prevents invalid class names at compile time.

API Route Example

type Version = 'v1' | 'v2';

type Route = `/api/${Version}/users`;

Allowed values:

'/api/v1/users'
'/api/v2/users'

Why use Template Literal Types?

  • Improve type safety for strings.
  • Avoid typing mistakes.
  • Generate combinations automatically.
  • Provide excellent IntelliSense.
  • Reduce duplicated string unions.

Template Literal Types vs String

stringTemplate Literal Type
Any string value.Only predefined combinations.
No autocomplete.Strong IntelliSense support.
Typos are allowed.Typos are caught at compile time.

Common Interview Questions

  • What are Template Literal Types? Types created by combining string literal types using template string syntax.
  • When would you use them? Event names, CSS classes, API routes, object keys, and naming conventions.
  • Can they work with unions? Yes. TypeScript generates every valid combination.
  • Can they use built-in utility types? Yes, including Capitalize, Uppercase, Lowercase, and Uncapitalize.
  • Do they exist at runtime? No. They are removed during compilation like other TypeScript types.

How to say it in an interview

"Template Literal Types let us generate strongly typed strings using template string syntax. They combine string literal types to produce valid string unions, making APIs safer and providing better IntelliSense. They're useful for event names, CSS classes, API routes, and naming conventions, and they work well with utility types like Capitalize and Uppercase."

question: How do you constrain generics using extends and keyof in TypeScript?
answer: Generic constraints limit the types that can be passed to a generic. The extends keyword ensures a type has a required shape, while keyof restricts a value to the valid property names of an object. Together, they enable type-safe and reusable generic functions.
tip: The combination T, K extends keyof T is one of the most frequently asked TypeScript interview patterns.
deep:
What are Generic Constraints?

Generic constraints allow you to restrict which types can be used with a generic. Instead of accepting every possible type, you can require the generic type to satisfy certain conditions.

The two most common ways are:

  • extends - Require a type to have a particular shape.
  • keyof - Restrict values to the keys of an object.

Using extends

The extends keyword ensures the generic type contains specific properties.

interface HasLength {
  length: number;
}

function printLength(value: T) {
  console.log(value.length);
}

Valid examples:

printLength('Hello');
printLength([1, 2, 3]);
printLength({ length: 10 });

Invalid example:

printLength(100);
// ❌ Error
// number does not have a length property

Using keyof

The keyof operator creates a union of all property names of an object.

interface User {
  id: number;
  name: string;
}

type Keys = keyof User;
// 'id' | 'name'

Combining extends and keyof

This is one of the most popular TypeScript interview questions.

function getProp(obj: T, key: K): T[K] {
  return obj[key];
}

Example:

const user = {
  id: 1,
  name: 'John'
};

getProp(user, 'name');
// 'John'

getProp(user, 'id');
// 1

// getProp(user, 'age');
// ❌ Error

The compiler prevents invalid property names.

Understanding T[K]

T[K] means 'the type of the selected property'.

const user = {
  id: 1,
  name: 'John'
};
ExpressionResult
T['id']number
T['name']string

Real-world Angular Example

interface User {
  id: number;
  name: string;
  email: string;
}

function getField(item: T, field: K): T[K] {
  return item[field];
}

const user: User = {
  id: 1,
  name: 'John',
  email: 'john@example.com'
};

const email = getField(user, 'email');

This pattern is commonly used in reusable Angular utility functions and libraries.

Why use Generic Constraints?

  • Prevents invalid types.
  • Provides better IntelliSense.
  • Catches errors during compilation.
  • Improves code reuse.
  • Makes utility functions type-safe.

Difference between extends and keyof

extendskeyof
Restricts the generic type.Returns the property names of a type.
Requires a specific structure.Restricts values to valid object keys.
Used for type constraints.Used for property access.

Common Interview Questions

  • What does extends do in generics? It restricts the generic to types that satisfy a required shape.
  • What does keyof return? A union of all property names of a type.
  • Why use K extends keyof T? It ensures only valid property names can be passed.
  • What does T[K] mean? The type of the selected property.
  • Where are generic constraints commonly used? Utility functions, reusable libraries, Angular helper methods, and API wrappers.

How to say it in an interview

"Generic constraints make generics safer by limiting the types they accept. The extends keyword requires a type to have a particular structure, while keyof restricts values to the valid property names of an object. A common interview pattern is K extends keyof T, which allows type-safe property access while preserving the correct return type using T[K]."
